past_medical_history: Iron deficiency anemia, treated and resolved
family_history: Mother: systemic lupus erythematosus, well controlled on hydroxychloroquine
No family history of epilepsy or psychiatric hospitalizations

social_history.recreational_drugs: Tried marijuana once in high school; denies recent use
social_history.living_situation: Lives in campus dormitory with a roommate
social_history.recent_stressors: Midterm examinations completed two weeks ago; recent breakup with boyfriend three weeks ago

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place but hesitates when asked the date, ultimately providing the correct month but incorrect day. Affect is flat with intermittent periods of inappropriate suspiciousness, glancing toward corners of the room and whispering that 'they can hear us.' Speech is fluent with normal rate and rhythm, no paraphasic errors, no word-finding difficulty on confrontation naming. Able to follow two-step commands but occasionally loses track mid-task. Registered 3 out of 3 words but recalled only 1 of 3 at five minutes. Clock drawing mildly disorganized with numbers clustered on the right side. Montreal Cognitive Assessment score estimated at 23/30 with deficits in delayed recall, attention (serial 7s), and visuospatial domains.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light, 3 mm bilaterally. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Face symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations. No orofacial dyskinesias observed during examination.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone normal in all four extremities. Strength 5/5 throughout in bilateral upper and lower extremities. No pronator drift. No involuntary movements noted during the examination.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception in all four extremities. No extinction to double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady, narrow-based gait. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: During the 45-minute encounter the patient had two brief episodes (each lasting approximately 5-8 seconds) of staring with behavioral arrest, during which she appeared to lose awareness, followed by immediate return to baseline without post-ictal confusion. These episodes were subtle and initially attributed to inattention. No tongue biting, incontinence, or tonic-clonic activity observed. The patient denied awareness of these episodes.

patient.chief_complaint: Bizarre behavior, paranoia, and insomnia for one week
patient.history_present_illness: This 22-year-old right-handed woman was brought to the emergency department by her mother and roommate after one week of progressively bizarre behavior. According to the roommate, the patient was entirely well and performing normally in her classes until approximately ten days ago, when she developed what appeared to be a mild cold with rhinorrhea and mild sore throat lasting two to three days. She did not seek medical attention for this and seemed to recover fully. Approximately seven days prior to presentation, the roommate noticed the patient had difficulty sleeping, staying up most of the night pacing in their shared room. Over the following days the patient became increasingly withdrawn, missed several classes -- which was highly uncharacteristic -- and began expressing paranoid ideations, telling her roommate that her professors were 'conspiring against her' and that her phone was being monitored. She stopped eating regularly and became irritable when questioned about her behavior. The roommate initially attributed this to stress from her recent breakup and upcoming final exams. Four days prior to presentation, the patient's mother called to check in and was alarmed by her daughter's disorganized speech and flat affect, which the mother described as 'not sounding like herself at all.' The mother drove from out of state and found her daughter's dorm room in disarray. The patient was wearing the same clothes she had been wearing days prior, had not showered, and was sitting on her bed staring at the wall. When her mother attempted to engage her, the patient alternated between flat unresponsiveness and agitated outbursts in which she accused her mother of being an imposter. The mother took the patient to an urgent care clinic two days prior to this presentation, where the patient was evaluated and referred to psychiatry with a preliminary diagnosis of acute psychotic episode, possibly related to her anxiety disorder. A prescription for risperidone 1 mg was provided but the patient refused to take it. Over the subsequent 48 hours her condition did not improve. The mother reports that last night the patient was found standing in the dormitory hallway at 3 AM talking to no one, and today the patient did not recognize her own roommate for several minutes before abruptly returning to baseline. No witnessed seizures, no head trauma, no recent travel, no tick bites, and no known illicit drug use. The patient herself provides a vague and inconsistent history, stating she feels fine but cannot explain her behavior.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8 Hz alpha rhythm present bilaterally, reactive to eye opening but mildly slowed for age

findings.frequency: 6-7 Hz theta range intermixed with the background
findings.state: Present during wakefulness, more prominent during drowsiness

findings.morphology: Rhythmic, moderate to high amplitude, intermittent bursts lasting 2-5 seconds
findings.state: Primarily during drowsiness, occasional runs during wakefulness

artifacts.description: Intermittent frontotemporal muscle artifact bilaterally during periods of jaw clenching

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Mildly increased background slowing during hyperventilation, returning to baseline post-procedure
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Symmetric photic driving at 8-14 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG for a 22-year-old showing mild diffuse background slowing and frontal intermittent rhythmic delta activity (FIRDA). No definite epileptiform discharges identified. No extreme delta brush pattern. The findings are nonspecific and may be seen in early encephalopathy of various etiologies including metabolic, toxic, or inflammatory causes. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording. Two brief clinical events of staring reported by the accompanying family during the recording but no clear electrographic correlate identified, possibly due to brevity and depth of ictal generator. Extended or continuous EEG monitoring is recommended.

findings.location: Two punctate foci in the deep periventricular white matter of the right frontal lobe

additional_observations: Ventricles normal in size and configuration
No extra-axial collections or subdural hematoma
Temporal lobes appear normal without signal abnormality
Hippocampi symmetric and normal in size bilaterally
mri.impression: Essentially normal brain MRI. Two punctate nonspecific T2/FLAIR hyperintense foci in the right frontal deep white matter without restricted diffusion or enhancement. These are of uncertain clinical significance and may represent incidental perivascular spaces or nonspecific gliosis. No findings to suggest encephalitis, demyelination, mass lesion, or acute vascular event. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Equivocal (2 faint bands, uncertain if distinct from serum)
special_tests.Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Sent to reference laboratory, results pending (estimated 7-10 business days)

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 16 cmH2O. WBC: 3 cells/uL (normal) (10% PMN/85% lymph). Protein: 37.6 mg/dL. Glucose: 58.1 mg/dL (ratio 0.67 (serum glucose 92 mg/dL)). Gram stain: No organisms seen. HSV-1 PCR: Negative. HSV-2 PCR: Negative. VZV PCR: Negative. Enterovirus PCR: Negative. Cytology: Rare lymphocytes, no malignant cells. Oligoclonal bands: Equivocal (2 faint bands, uncertain if distinct from serum). IgG index: 0.68 (borderline, normal <0.70).

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Serum anti-NMDA receptor IgG Weakly positive (titer 1:10)  (H), CSF anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:32)  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Serum anti-NMDA receptor IgG: Weakly positive (titer 1:10) (L)

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: Normal, 2.9 x 2.0 x 2.2 cm, contains a dominant follicle measuring 1.5 cm consistent with normal menstrual cycle. No solid masses.
3.1 x 2.3 x 2.4 cm, contains a 1.2 cm complex lesion with a small echogenic focus and predominantly cystic component. Internal vascularity minimal on Doppler.
Normal size and echotexture, endometrial stripe 8 mm consistent with secretory phase
Small amount of physiologic free fluid in cul-de-sac

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Left ovary 3.1 x 2.3 x 2.4 cm, contains a 1.2 cm complex lesion with a small echogenic focus and predominantly cystic component. Internal vascularity minimal on Doppler.  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Left ovary: 3.1 x 2.3 x 2.4 cm, contains a 1.2 cm complex lesion with a small echogenic focus and predominantly cystic component. Internal vascularity minimal on Doppler. (H)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Anti-NMDA receptor Ab (CSF) Positive (1:32)  (H).

findings.description: density: Mixed density with a small focus of fat attenuation (-30 HU) and a tiny calcific focus; enhancement: No significant enhancement

additional_observations: Small amount of physiologic pelvic free fluid
output.impression: No evidence of other primary malignancy, lymphadenopathy, or metastatic disease. Given the clinical diagnosis of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis, this teratoma is likely the immunologic trigger and should be resected. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (4 unique bands not present in paired serum)
special_tests.Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Positive (titer 1:64, increased from 1:32 on initial LP)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 20 cmH2O. WBC: 12 cells/uL (elevated) (5% PMN/92% lymph). Protein: 48.7 mg/dL. Glucose: 62.3 mg/dL (ratio 0.64 (serum glucose 92 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Positive (4 unique bands not present in paired serum). IgG index: 0.78 (elevated, normal <0.70). Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Positive (titer 1:64, increased from 1:32 on initial LP). Cytology: Reactive lymphocytes, no malignant cells.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7-8 Hz, slowed compared to routine EEG from admission

findings.type: Generalized rhythmic delta activity with superimposed beta
findings.frequency: 1.5-2 Hz delta with superimposed 20-25 Hz beta bursts
findings.morphology: Emerging pattern of rhythmic delta with overriding fast activity
findings.state: Primarily during sleep but emerging in wakefulness
findings.clinical_correlation: This pattern is evolving toward extreme delta brush, which is pathognomonic for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. The pattern may not be fully developed in early disease.

findings.frequency: One seizure captured during 48-hour recording, lasting 35 seconds
findings.morphology: Rhythmic sharp theta evolving to repetitive sharp waves with secondary generalization
findings.state: Occurred during sleep, no clinical correlate identified
findings.clinical_correlation: Subclinical seizure confirms epileptogenic process. Combined with the emerging delta brush pattern, this is highly suggestive of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis.

findings.state: Worsened compared to initial routine EEG

artifacts.description: Intermittent movement artifact during periods of agitation

output.impression: Moderately abnormal continuous EEG recording over 48 hours showing progressive background slowing compared to initial routine EEG, one subclinical electrographic seizure of left frontotemporal origin, and an emerging pattern of generalized rhythmic delta with superimposed beta activity that may represent early or developing extreme delta brush. This electrographic evolution is highly suggestive of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and is consistent with disease progression.
recommended_actions: Initiate immunotherapy without waiting for antibody results given the clinical trajectory and EEG evolution
ICU level of care for monitoring

Psychiatric Consultation Note.value: The presentation is atypical for a primary psychiatric disorder. While paranoid ideation and disorganized behavior are present, several features raise concern for an organic etiology: (1) no prior psychiatric history beyond mild anxiety, (2) acute onset over days rather than a prodromal period of months typical for schizophrenia spectrum disorders, (3) subtle cognitive deficits on bedside testing atypical for first-break psychosis, (4) brief staring episodes concerning for possible seizures, (5) family history of autoimmune disease (SLE, Graves disease) which increases the risk of autoimmune encephalitis. Recommend thorough medical workup before attributing symptoms to a primary psychiatric disorder. Anti-NMDA receptor antibody testing is specifically recommended.

While paranoid ideation and disorganized behavior are present, several features raise concern for an organic etiology: (1) no prior psychiatric history beyond mild anxiety, (2) acute onset over days rather than a prodromal period of months typical for schizophrenia spectrum disorders, (3) subtle cognitive deficits on bedside testing atypical for first-break psychosis, (4) brief staring episodes concerning for possible seizures, (5) family history of autoimmune disease (SLE, Graves disease) which increases the risk of autoimmune encephalitis. Recommend thorough medical workup before attributing symptoms to a primary psychiatric disorder. Anti-NMDA receptor antibody testing is specifically recommended.  (H).

While paranoid ideation and disorganized behavior are present, several features raise concern for an organic etiology: (1) no prior psychiatric history beyond mild anxiety, (2) acute onset over days rather than a prodromal period of months typical for schizophrenia spectrum disorders, (3) subtle cognitive deficits on bedside testing atypical for first-break psychosis, (4) brief staring episodes concerning for possible seizures, (5) family history of autoimmune disease (SLE, Graves disease) which increases the risk of autoimmune encephalitis. Recommend thorough medical workup before attributing symptoms to a primary psychiatric disorder. Anti-NMDA receptor antibody testing is specifically recommended. (H)

differential.diagnosis: Primary psychiatric disorder (first-break psychosis / brief psychotic disorder)
differential.key_distinguishing: True first-break psychosis typically has a prodromal period of weeks to months with gradual social withdrawal, no seizure-like episodes, normal EEG, no cognitive decline on formal testing. This patient has acute onset, subtle cognitive deficits on MoCA, and brief staring episodes suggestive of subclinical seizures.

HSV encephalitis typically presents with fever, acute temporal lobe symptoms (aphasia, memory loss), and MRI showing unilateral temporal lobe edema with hemorrhagic changes. This patient is afebrile with a normal MRI and negative CSF HSV PCR.

Requires markedly elevated anti-TPO antibodies and typically fluctuating encephalopathy with steroid responsiveness. This patient has normal thyroid function and normal anti-TPO antibodies. Family history of autoimmune thyroid disease is noted but does not confirm this diagnosis.
Urine drug screen is negative. No history of recent substance use. Onset does not correlate with known drug exposure timeline.

differential.diagnosis: Systemic lupus erythematosus with CNS involvement (neuropsychiatric lupus)
differential.key_distinguishing: Family history of SLE and borderline ANA could raise suspicion, but patient has no systemic lupus manifestations (rash, arthritis, serositis, cytopenias). ANA at 1:40 is nonspecific and below diagnostic threshold.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive initial workup: CBC, BMP, hepatic panel, thyroid function, CRP, ESR, CK, ammonia, urine drug screen, blood alcohol level
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Largely normal labs with borderline low sodium as the only abnormality; negative drug screen

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal or near-normal MRI; nonspecific white matter foci; no findings diagnostic of encephalitis

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, cultures, viral PCRs (HSV, VZV, enterovirus), oligoclonal bands, IgG index, and critically: anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Initially near-normal CSF, but antibodies sent to reference lab (key diagnostic step even when CSF appears normal)
Mild diffuse slowing and FIRDA -- nonspecific but should prompt suspicion for organic encephalopathy rather than primary psychiatric disorder

optimal_actions.action: Recognize red flags for organic etiology despite psychiatric-appearing presentation: acute onset, cognitive deficits, staring episodes, preceding viral prodrome, family autoimmune history. Request psychiatric consultation to assess if presentation is atypical for primary psychosis.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Psychiatry agrees presentation is atypical for primary psychiatric disorder

optimal_actions.action: Order continuous EEG monitoring for at least 24-48 hours given concern for subclinical seizures based on staring episodes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Evolving pattern toward extreme delta brush, subclinical electrographic seizure captured, confirming organic process

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h pending viral PCR results to cover for possible HSV encephalitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Empiric coverage while awaiting PCR results; can be discontinued once HSV PCR returns negative

optimal_actions.action: When antibody results return positive or if clinical suspicion is high based on disease trajectory and EEG evolution: initiate first-line immunotherapy with IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily x 5 days AND IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical stabilization and beginning of improvement, though full recovery may take weeks to months

optimal_actions.action: Order pelvic ultrasound to screen for ovarian teratoma
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Small complex ovarian lesion suspicious for teratoma requiring further characterization

optimal_actions.action: If clinical deterioration continues despite first-line immunotherapy, consider repeat lumbar puncture and escalation to second-line therapy (rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly x 4 doses)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Repeat LP shows evolving CSF inflammatory profile with rising antibody titers

optimal_actions.action: Initiate anti-seizure medication (levetiracetam 500 mg IV/PO q12h) if seizures are confirmed on continuous EEG
Consult gynecologic surgery for teratoma resection once confirmed
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Surgical planning for tumor removal to eliminate the immunologic trigger

critical_actions: Send CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies even when initial CSF appears normal -- this is the single most important diagnostic step in this puzzle case
Do not accept a primary psychiatric diagnosis without ruling out autoimmune encephalitis, especially in a young woman with acute-onset psychosis

Recognize subtle clinical red flags: brief staring episodes (possible subclinical seizures), cognitive deficits disproportionate to psychiatric presentation, preceding viral prodrome, and autoimmune family history
Start empiric acyclovir until HSV PCR returns negative
Order continuous EEG monitoring when routine EEG shows nonspecific abnormalities and clinical suspicion for seizures exists
Screen for ovarian teratoma once diagnosis is confirmed or strongly suspected
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing as primary psychiatric disorder and initiating antipsychotic therapy without completing a neurological workup including CSF anti-NMDAR antibody testing
Administering high-dose antipsychotics such as haloperidol, which can worsen movement disorders and trigger neuroleptic malignant syndrome in anti-NMDAR encephalitis patients
Discharging the patient with only outpatient psychiatry follow-up based on the initial normal labs and imaging
Accepting the normal initial CSF as definitively ruling out autoimmune encephalitis
Delaying immunotherapy while waiting weeks for reference laboratory antibody results if clinical and electrographic trajectory is worsening
Rapid sodium correction if hyponatremia worsens, due to risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome
key_reasoning_points: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis frequently presents initially with isolated psychiatric symptoms (paranoia, psychosis, personality change) before neurological features like seizures, dyskinesias, and autonomic instability develop -- this early window is the diagnostic puzzle
A normal initial CSF does not exclude anti-NMDAR encephalitis; up to 20% of cases have normal CSF early in the disease course, and antibodies may take days to become detectable
Approximately 50% of anti-NMDAR encephalitis cases have a normal brain MRI, making imaging unreliable as a screening test for this diagnosis
The preceding viral prodrome (URI-like illness) is a recognized feature of anti-NMDAR encephalitis and should raise clinical suspicion in the context of subsequent neuropsychiatric decline
Subtle EEG abnormalities (mild diffuse slowing, FIRDA) in a patient with apparent psychiatric symptoms should prompt consideration of organic encephalopathy and extended EEG monitoring
Family history of autoimmune disease (SLE, Graves disease) increases the pre-test probability of autoimmune encephalitis and should lower the threshold for antibody testing
Brief staring episodes in the context of acute psychosis should raise concern for subclinical seizures, which are common in anti-NMDAR encephalitis and rare in primary psychiatric disorders
The borderline low sodium (134 mEq/L) is an easily overlooked but important early clue to SIADH, which occurs in anti-NMDAR encephalitis due to hypothalamic dysfunction

metadata.diagnostic_challenge_notes: This case is designed as a diagnostic puzzle where the initial presentation mimics a primary psychiatric disorder. Nearly all initial workup results (labs, MRI, CSF) are normal or near-normal, making the diagnosis extremely easy to miss. The key to solving this puzzle is recognizing the subtle red flags and having a high index of suspicion for autoimmune encephalitis in young women with acute-onset psychosis.
